name: A scheduled task was created
applicable_version: Windows Server 2016 and later; Windows 10 and later
definition: Windows Security Event ID 4698 records creation of a scheduled task and supports detection of task-based persistence and execution.
trigger_scenarios: The event is written when a scheduled task is registered and Task Scheduler auditing is enabled.

key_fields.explanation: The task path and name. Hidden-looking names, names that imitate Microsoft tasks, and new tasks on servers are useful pivots.
The XML task definition includes command, arguments, trigger, principal, and run level.
The creating account. Review whether its role and logon context authorize task creation.

false_positives: Enterprise management agents and software updaters create scheduled tasks.
Administrators and developers may create one-time maintenance tasks.
detection_notes: T1053.005 is concrete when Task Content sets <Command>powershell.exe</Command>, mshta.exe, or a C:\\Users\\ / C:\\ProgramData\\ path, or sets <RunLevel>HighestAvailable</RunLevel> or SYSTEM. Those XML values establish executable persistence, not merely an unusual Task Name.
kql_snippet:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4698
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, Account, TaskName, TaskContent
| order by TimeGenerated desc
spl_snippet:
index=wineventlog EventCode=4698
| table _time, host, SubjectUserName, TaskName, TaskContent
sample_log:
Task Name: \Microsoft\Windows\Update\CacheRefresh
Task Content: <Exec><Command>powershell.exe</Command><Arguments>-File C:\Users\Public\refresh.ps1</Arguments></Exec>
Subject: CORP\admin.ops

queries.title: Retrieve Event ID 4698
queries.query:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4698
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, Account, EventData
| order by TimeGenerated desc
assumptions: The Microsoft Sentinel Windows Security Events connector populates SecurityEvent.
Apply the event-specific field and correlation guidance on this page after retrieving the base events.

queries.title: Retrieve Event ID 4698
queries.query:
index=wineventlog source="WinEventLog:Security" EventCode=4698
| table _time, host, user, EventCode, Message
assumptions: Index, source, and field aliases vary by Splunk deployment and Windows add-on version.
Map provider fields from the raw event before implementing event-specific filtering or aggregation.

queries.title: Retrieve Event ID 4698
assumptions: The relevant Windows event channel is ingested through a Cortex XDR Windows event collection path.
Inspect raw provider fields because parsed field names vary by collection path and event provider.

queries.title: Windows Event ID 4698
queries.query:
title: Windows Event ID 4698
status: experimental
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: security
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 4698
    condition: selection
falsepositives:
    - Legitimate administrative or system activity
level: informational
assumptions: This rule retrieves the event and is not a standalone malicious-behavior detection.
Add the field conditions and correlations documented on this page in the destination SIEM.

value_references.meaning: The task requests the highest privilege level available to its principal.
value_references.security_relevance: Pair this value with the task principal and executable path to identify elevated persistence rather than treating the value alone as malicious.

faqs.question: What does Windows Event ID 4698 mean?
faqs.answer: Event ID 4698 records creation of a scheduled task and includes the task name and XML task definition.

faqs.question: Where is the executable stored in Event ID 4698?
faqs.answer: Inspect the TaskContent XML, especially Exec Command and Arguments elements; other task action types can appear and should also be reviewed.

faqs.question: Are all newly created scheduled tasks suspicious?
faqs.answer: No. Windows, management tools, and applications create legitimate tasks; prioritize unexpected principals, elevated run levels, unusual triggers, and commands in user-writable or temporary paths.

sources.title: 4698: Microsoft Windows security auditing event reference
